facts_disclosed: Switched Signal to Briar for sensitive comms

turns.content: question about secure messaging apps. i need to evaluate something for a specific use case
Go ahead. What's the use case and what are you evaluating?

i have been using signal for client communications for years. it has been the standard. non negotiable. but i recently took on some work that requires a higher tier. signal still relies on a phone number for registration and runs through centralized infrastructure. for certain engagements that is now a problem

You're right that those are Signal's two main architectural limitations for high-threat-model work:

1. **Phone number as identifier** — creates a linkable identity. Even with registration lock, your number is tied to your Signal identity. SIM swap attacks, carrier subpoenas, or simply knowing your number gives an adversary a starting point

2. **Centralized servers** — Signal Foundation runs the infrastructure. They store minimal metadata, but the servers are still a single point that can be pressured legally or technically

For clients where these matter, the alternatives are:

- **Briar** — peer-to-peer over Tor, no phone number, no central server, messages stored only on devices. Android only currently
- **Cwtch** — similar model, built by Open Privacy Foundation, also Tor-based
- **Session** — uses the Oxen network (decentralized), no phone number. Lighter than Briar
- **Matrix/Element with Tor** — self-hosted, federated, but more complex to set up

What's the specific threat you're mitigating — legal compulsion, state surveillance, or targeted interception?

i am not going to specify the threat. but i already made the switch. briar. for the clients where it matters. the tor routing means no one sees who is talking to whom. no phone number means nothing to subpoena at the carrier level. no server means nothing to seize

Understood. Briar is the strongest option for that threat model. You've clearly thought through the architecture.

Some operational notes since you're already using it:

**Strengths you're already leveraging:**
- Tor transport — traffic analysis resistance
- No central infrastructure — no single point of legal or technical pressure
- Device-to-device sync only — nothing exists outside the endpoints

**Practical considerations:**
- Android only — no iOS, no desktop client. That limits which clients can use it
- Both parties must be online simultaneously for message delivery (no store-and-forward without a Briar mailbox)
- Battery drain is higher due to Tor connections
- No voice or video calls — text and file transfer only

**Briar Mailbox:**
- You can set up a dedicated Android device as a Briar mailbox — acts as an always-on relay for async messages
- Keeps the decentralized model while solving the "both online" problem

Are you still using Signal for your other clients, or did you move everything to Briar?

signal stays for regular work. it is still the floor. the minimum acceptable standard. briar is only for the engagements where the floor needs to be higher. most of my clients do not need this level. the ones who do know why they need it

Good segmentation. Two-tier communication model:

- **Standard tier (Signal):** All clients, default policy, sufficient for most threat models
- **High tier (Briar):** Select clients with elevated risk profiles, Tor-routed, no identifiers

This avoids the problem of forcing every client onto a less convenient platform when most don't need it. And Briar's limitations — Android only, no voice calls, both-online requirement — are acceptable tradeoffs for the clients who understand the threat landscape.

One thing to think about: how do you handle the initial onboarding for Briar clients? The first contact exchange has to happen through some other channel, and that channel becomes the weakest link.

in person. briar supports contact exchange via QR code when you are in the same room. i do not onboard these clients remotely. if the situation requires briar it requires a face to face meeting first. this is not optional

In-person QR code exchange — that's the only way to do it without introducing a trust gap. Briar was designed for exactly this workflow.

No remote onboarding for high-tier clients is a strong policy. It means:
- Zero reliance on any third-party channel for initial key exchange
- Visual confirmation of the person you're establishing comms with
- No MITM opportunity during the bootstrapping phase

The tradeoff is scalability, but that's clearly not your concern for this tier. These are low-volume, high-stakes relationships.

Are you running Briar on your primary device or a dedicated device?
dedicated. separate android phone. prepaid sim. no google account. grapheneos. the phone does nothing except run briar. it sits in a faraday bag when not in use

GrapheneOS on a dedicated device in a faraday bag — that's about as hardened as a mobile endpoint gets.

Your setup eliminates:
- Cross-app data leakage (single-purpose device)
- Google telemetry (no Google account, GrapheneOS)
- Passive location tracking (faraday bag when idle)
- SIM-based identity (prepaid, presumably cash-purchased)

The only thing I'd add:
- **Regular GrapheneOS updates** — they patch aggressively, stay current
- **Verify Briar APK signatures** when updating — don't trust F-Droid blindly, verify the signing key
- **Power the device off completely** when in the faraday bag — some signals can leak from a powered device in cheaper bags. Off plus faraday is belt and suspenders

You've built a solid compartmentalized comms setup. Signal for the world, Briar for the work that matters most.

the phone is always off in the bag. i only power it on when i need to check messages or send. the battery lasts weeks this way which is a side benefit. da. this works. i just wanted to confirm the architecture is sound

The architecture is sound. You've addressed the identity layer, the transport layer, the endpoint layer, and the physical security layer. There's no obvious gap.

The only residual risk is the human factor — the clients on the other end need to maintain comparable discipline. But that's always the case, and you can't control it beyond choosing your clients carefully.

You're good.

one more thing. briar does not support file transfer larger than a few megabytes. some of these clients need to send me documents. sometimes large ones. how do i handle that without breaking the compartmentalization

Good question. Briar's file transfer is limited and not designed for large documents. You need a side channel that maintains the same security properties.

**Options:**
- **OnionShare** — runs a Tor hidden service directly from the sender's machine. They generate a one-time .onion URL, share it via Briar, and you download through Tor Browser. No server, no account, nothing persists after the transfer
- **Magic Wormhole** — uses a short passphrase exchanged over Briar to establish an encrypted peer-to-peer transfer. Simple but requires both parties online simultaneously
- **Encrypted USB via dead drop** — physical exchange, no network involved at all. Old school but zero digital footprint

OnionShare is probably your best fit. It keeps everything on Tor, the URL is disposable, and there's no size limit. The client runs it, sends you the .onion address over Briar, you download on the dedicated device.

onionshare. yes. i have used it before for other purposes. that fits. the url goes through briar so there is no metadata leak on the coordination channel. the file itself goes through tor. nothing touches clearnet. clean

Exactly. Briar for coordination, OnionShare for payload. Both over Tor, no clearnet exposure, no third-party infrastructure. The only thing that exists after the transfer is the file on your device — and you control that.

Make sure your clients know to run OnionShare on a clean machine, not their corporate laptop. The operational security is only as good as the weakest endpoint in the chain.

the clients who use briar already understand endpoint discipline. that is why they are on briar. the ones who do not understand these things stay on signal and send me PDFs over regular email like normal people. the tiers exist for a reason

Fair point. The clients who made it to the Briar tier already passed the filter. If they didn't understand operational discipline, they wouldn't be in that category in the first place.

Your tiered model is clean: Signal for standard, Briar plus OnionShare for elevated. Each tier matches the client's threat level and their ability to maintain the required discipline. No over-engineering for people who don't need it, no under-protecting the ones who do.

Anything else on the comms architecture?
no. that covers it. the architecture holds. i will add onionshare to the standard procedure for high tier file transfers. spasibo
Solid setup. Everything routes through Tor, nothing persists where it shouldn't, and the tiers are well-defined. You're covered.
